subject: ap-government
title: SCOTUS Comparison — Cohen v. California (1971)
stimulus:
In 1968 Paul Cohen wore a jacket displaying an obscene anti-war statement while entering a Los Angeles courthouse. Cohen was arrested and charged under a California statute that prohibited "maliciously and willfully disturb[ing] the peace and quiet of any neighborhood or person [by] offensive conduct." Cohen asserted that he wore the jacket in protest of the Vietnam War. Cohen was convicted in a Los Angeles court and sentenced to 30 days in jail.

In the subsequent case Cohen v. California (1971), the Court ruled in a 5–4 decision in favor of Cohen, overturning his conviction. In the majority opinion, Justice John Marshall Harlan rejected the argument that the state could restrict the words on Cohen's jacket as offensive conduct. Harlan reasoned that "[O]ne man's vulgarity is another's lyric," warning that "government might soon seize upon the censorship of particular words as a convenient guise for banning the expression of unpopular views."

parts.prompt: Identify the civil liberty that is common to both Schenck v. United States (1919) and Cohen v. California (1971).
parts.scoring_notes: Accept 'freedom of speech,' 'free speech,' or 'First Amendment speech rights.' Only freedom of speech earns the point — freedom of the press or religion do not.

scoring_points.description: Identify the civil liberty that is common to both Schenck v. United States (1919) and Cohen v. California (1971).

required_elements: Identifies freedom of speech (or First Amendment free speech) as the civil liberty common to both cases
alternatives.correct_example: Freedom of speech is the civil liberty common to both Schenck v. United States and Cohen v. California.

wrong_examples: Freedom of religion — neither case involved religious expression.
Freedom of the press — Cohen wore a jacket, not published writing; Schenck distributed pamphlets but the issue was speech content, not press freedom.
common_traps: Accept 'First Amendment rights' only if the student clearly means speech — vague 'First Amendment rights' without specifying speech is borderline
Do not accept 'freedom of expression' unless it specifically refers to speech in this context

parts.prompt: Explain how the facts in Schenck v. United States and Cohen v. California resulted in different holdings.
parts.scoring_notes: First point requires accurate description of Schenck (the required case) — either the facts or the holding. Second point requires connecting the factual differences to the different outcomes: Schenck's speech posed a direct threat to national security during wartime, while Cohen's offensive jacket was merely distasteful and posed no concrete danger.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the facts in Schenck v. United States and Cohen v. California resulted in different holdings. (1 point — describe relevant fact or holding about the required Supreme Court case Schenck v. United States)

required_elements: Accurately describes a fact or holding from Schenck v. United States, the required case
alternatives.correct_example: In Schenck v. United States, Charles Schenck was arrested for distributing pamphlets urging men to resist the draft during World War I, in violation of the Espionage Act.

required_elements: Accurately describes a fact or holding from Schenck v. United States, the required case
alternatives.correct_example: The Supreme Court upheld Schenck's conviction, holding that the First Amendment does not protect speech that presents a clear and present danger to public welfare, such as obstructing military recruitment during wartime.

wrong_examples: Describes only Cohen without mentioning Schenck — earns 0 regardless of accuracy.
Confuses Schenck with Tinker or another First Amendment case.
common_traps: Only the Schenck information is needed for the first point — comparison is not required at this tier
Both factual description and the holding earn the point

scoring_points.description: Explain how the facts in Schenck v. United States and Cohen v. California resulted in different holdings. (2 points — correctly explains how facts of BOTH cases led to different holdings)

required_elements: Explains the key factual difference between both cases that caused the different First Amendment outcomes
alternatives.correct_example: In Schenck, distributing anti-draft pamphlets during wartime posed a clear and present danger to national security, so the Court held the speech was not protected. In Cohen, wearing a jacket with an offensive slogan was merely distasteful — it presented no concrete threat to public safety — so the Court held that the government could not punish Cohen for his political expression.

wrong_examples: States only that 'both involved free speech but had different outcomes' — does not explain why the outcomes differed.
Describes only Schenck in detail without connecting to Cohen's facts.
common_traps: Must explain why the facts led to different outcomes — not just identify that the outcomes were different
The core distinction is threatening/dangerous speech vs. merely offensive speech

parts.prompt: Explain how the decision in Cohen v. California reflects the democratic ideal of limited government.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain the connection between the Cohen ruling and limited government — specifically that the ruling constrains governmental power to censor expression deemed offensive, thus limiting the state's authority over individual expression.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the decision in Cohen v. California reflects the democratic ideal of limited government.

required_elements: Connects the Cohen ruling to limited government by explaining how it restricts governmental power to censor offensive speech
alternatives.correct_example: The decision in Cohen reflects limited government because it prevents the state from using its power to silence speech simply because it finds the content offensive. By ruling that California could not punish Cohen for his jacket, the Court drew a boundary on government authority over individual expression.

required_elements: Explains that the ruling protected individual rights against overreach by a state government, demonstrating limited government
alternatives.correct_example: Limited government means the government's power to restrict individual liberty has defined limits. The Cohen decision upheld this ideal by prohibiting California from enforcing a law that effectively allowed the government to police the content of political messages — an area where government power must be constrained.

wrong_examples: Simply stating that 'Cohen won' without explaining the limited government concept.
Describing federalism or the Commerce Clause, which are unrelated to the limited government question here.
common_traps: The response must explain limited government as a concept and link it to the specific Cohen holding — not just mention the term
Accept any response that accurately explains how the ruling constrains government authority over speech, even if it uses different terminology
